body:
The `updateLibrary` is a high-level API, same as `updateScene`, which also takes `appState` (which can alter UI state) and `commitToHistory`. It's about usefulness and semantics. Since opening the library menu is currently tied to updating the library, it makes sense to expose it.

The alternative is to force host apps to do an extra:

```
excalidrawAPI.updateScene({ appState: { isLibraryOpen: true } });
```

I don't care much, but I think the argument here and in your other comments stems from wanting to make this API low-level, which we don't need to.
author: dwelle

body:
We have a problem with grapheme clusters or multi-codepoint-letters or whatever you want to call them. The core issue with the current code is that  `[...'🤌🏼👌🏿']` evaluates to `[ '🤌', '🏼', '👌', '🏿' ]` 

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9257001/167246490-05ecd607-74e9-4d6d-ab9a-741cbb0d01a6.mov
author: tom-sherman

body:
I'm assuming that the text selection API also works on graphemes ie. selecting `🤌🏼` gives you a range of length 1 not 2. If this is the case I think the only solution is to have both `textElement.colorRanges` and `getLineGroupedRanges` work off of graphemes too.

In any case somewhere is going to have to be responsible for splitting by grapheme which probably means we need to pull in https://github.com/orling/grapheme-splitter

Edit: Text selection doesn't work on graphemes. Selecting any standard emoji gives you a range of length 2.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Ah, we should use https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Intl/Segmenter

Would need to include a polyfill for firefox tho, not sure on the strategy of supporty/polyfills in this repo. Can anyone comment?
author: tom-sherman

body:
haven't reviewed yet so unclear what issues you're facing exactly, but do we even need to be aware of graphemes? Can't we just save the exact offsets of a given selection range? I assume the answer is we can't mainly due to how it would work alongside line breaks when bound text comes into play?

If not, then the key is not to use array spread `[...string]` because it splits on code points (thus is potentially multi-byte), and instead use `string.split('')` which splits on bytes — then you can just index into the string as normal.
author: dwelle

body: The problem with doing `.split('')` here is that once they're split it's not clear how to recombine the codepoints to form the original graphemes. This is also why we need to be aware of graphemes in the first place, not sure you can apply a colour range without worrying about them because a range applies to graphemes - not codepoints.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Depending on what you do afterwards, you should still be able to treat the string as a black box and it won't really matter?

For example, if you have `Peru 🇵🇪`, and the user selected the flag emoji, the range would be `[5, 9]` (the flag is 4 bytes). You split which gets you:

```
const bytes = ['P', 'e', 'r', 'u', ' ', '\uD83C', '\uDDF5', '\uD83C', '\uDDEA']
```

You extract the flag by slicing it out:

```
const selection = bytes.slice(5, 9);
```

You can then `selection.join('')` which gets you the 🇵🇪 grapheme back.
author: dwelle

body:
this function must be optimized as it can take 150+ ms per frame for 50-point line (common in libraries) when called in the pointermove handler to determine which (mid)point you're hovering over — which causes noticeable lag, even race conditions where points tend to stay in hover state even after you move the cursor away.

If we can't make the algo better, e.g. narrow down to the segment the cursor is in instead of computing cursor distance to every line segment center, then we'll need to precalculate all segment midpoints and cache on line editor construction (it would also need to update on point move/delete/add/etc.).

body:
I agree with both of you. We definitely need to fix this issue. But having two props for theme (`props.theme` and `props.defaultTheme`) is not great, even though `defaultTheme` does solve this problem.

What we want is the ability to have the theme controlled (`props.theme`) but also show the UI toggle, _and_ still have the DX ok-ish.

So I suggest we keep the `props.theme` behavior as is, but make the (already existing) `props.UIOptions.canvasOptions.theme` to work in this way: default to `undefined` which means we'll render the theme toggle if `props.theme` is `undefined`. If `false`, never render it. If `true`, always render it regardless of `props.theme` existence.

We'll also want to factor out the theme persistence from `appState.theme` (`STORAGE_KEYS.LOCAL_STORAGE_APP_STATE`) to `theme` (`STORAGE_KEYS.THEME`). Not strictly necessary, but we want to be able to quickly access the theme without having to import the whole state (`importFromLocalStorage()`). App theme is semantically separate from editor theme anyway.

body:
The `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState.segmentMidpoint` should be part of global `pointerDownState`, or possibly the whole `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState` should be. Because if we're updating `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState` in a way that requires it to be saved back to `appState` (instead of mutating it), there's a race condition unless we `flushSync` it everywhere we update the linear editor — but even then I'm sure it'd be error prone.

The whole flow we're handling state during pointerdown/pointermove is super brittle and subject to race conditions right now. This manifests in things like:

![excal-midpoint-creation-loop](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5153846/204149460-2bdd463c-5bf3-48e2-a68b-628427330f82.gif)

Wrapping these calls into `flushSync` seems to fix it, but there are many other places where we do not set (linear editor) state synchronously, so those places may be subject to race conditions as well.
author: dwelle

body:
what's the reason for filtering out MenuGroups? At best it'll slow things down, and at worst it'll prevent nested MenuGroups.

That said, right now the reason for `<MenuGroup>` compo is only so that apps can render a group of items since we filter every non-whitelisted nodes out in `getValidMenuChildren()` (that "arbitrary" gap on the group should be removed, and added in host).

I'd retain the top-level filtering (and thus `MenuGroup` compo) only if we decide to have a hybrid solution where we render default items together with custom ones (where if people wanted not to render a default item, they'd need to render it and pass `render={false}` to it) — in which case having named components will be necessary (almost). This would serve as a solution to #6023 case 1 (the problem of host apps having to default items in sync) instead of having "default blocks"). But then we'd either have to recursively gather children (going down into MenuGroups) or tell host apps that if they want to put default menu items inside MenuGroups then they also need to render them in top-level with `render={false}` prop. They'd potentially have to do it anyway in case they'll render into submenus (once we support them).

Otherwise can let hosts render whatever they want.

Yeah, but we can't start breaking the component contract if you're rendering something tiny different from a plain string inside the item.

We also don't want to be highlighting the item if it's not really a list item, as we're doing with the `<LanguageList/>` item.

So I'm wondering if `DropdownMenu` is the right choice here, and whether we don't want a plain `Popover`.

Right now this is an internal implementation as we don't expose the DropdownMenu to users, so we can refactor later. But we'll need to come up with a public API for the menu items so that users can either use an item that should act as a list item (what `DropdownMenuItem` does right now when you supply a plain string) and when it should.. do almost nothing at all I suppose (I'm not sure what Menu styling/behavior we want to retain on items like our LanguageList item)?

---

I think for now we'll tell users that if they want to use items other than `MainMenu.Item`, then they should use `MainMenu.Group` or whatever they want. And we'll document what classes they should use to maintain padding/gaps/etc. correctly.

body:
Thanks for decoupling #6080 from #6037, it makes it easier to reason about the implementation! 

Here is the tl;dr of all the referenced PRs since it took me a while to wrap my head around:
- #6080 - registers additional custom actions to be called from the context menu; removes existing actions by name.
- #5983 - exposes existing actions to be called externally
- #6008 - exposes custom context menu renderer function to have full control over UI and actions content/order.

In my view listed above PRs complement each other by serving different use cases and can be treated/merged separately.

this is hardcoding all custom actions to be rendered in the properties sidebar which is arbitrary. And we will be removing component rendering from actions anyway: https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/6075

body:
Sure.

Please refer to https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/6483#issuecomment-1521566179 for the gist of it and some of the preceding discussion for context. The TL;DR is that we want to allow submenu nesting on mobile too and as such we don't want a full-width menu on mobile. We also don't want to allow clicks on canvas while the mobile menu is open, as it can be especially aggravating when the welcome screen is shown and you "fat finger click" behind the menu and open something you never intended to. So the solution we agreed on with @dwelle was that:
- on mobile, stick with a non full width main menu to allow nesting/submenus
- on mobile, show a backdrop while the main menu is open; clicking on the backdrop should close the menu
- on desktop, no such restriction is needed

Can you elaborate on this ?

> One other thing that may not happen now, but could happen later: we're not stripping the custom properties when we spread the objects.

Where I am calling `newElement`, `newLinearElement` etc APIs there it strips off so we need not worry however there are two places where I am not doing thats regular text and shapes (rectangle, ellipse, diamond) and we are not supporting any custom attributes in these cases so it doesn't matter but I can strip of custom attributes if passed mistakenly eg passing `start` for `rectangle`

body:
> Can you elaborate on this ?

https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/06dd6de0aff19f1728d55fee6312a3d5d6e6d03d/src/data/transform.ts#L545-L550

Granted, above it doesn't matter because we're reusing the width/height from the user supplied element, but if we later add something more, we may miss it.

But, there are other cases where when we calculate e.g. the wrapped text, you always prefer that calculated text. And I agree. That's why I think we should also prefer calculated `width`/`height` and other properties and not let them be overridden by the user.

> [...] we are not supporting any custom attributes in these cases so it doesn't matter but I can strip of custom attributes if passed mistakenly eg passing `start` for `rectangle`

Yep, hence why I said it may not happen now, but later (when we introduce custom props to generic or other elements). 

If we only care about the editor, `restore()` will take care of it. But if we care about the return type of the transformer, we may want to pass the generic elements through `newElement()` as well.

body: was this intentional? I don't think it makes sense for embeddable elements to support binding text.
author: dwelle

body:
It is intentional. If the embeddable has a bound text element that will be used as the alt-text when exporting to PNG or SVG. This could be enhanced further in the future to be less of a hidden feature... Right now there are two ways you can edit the text:
1) by creating the alt-text in a separate text element, then binding it. If you want to change it, you need to unbind, change the text, and bind again.
2) you can add text like to a rectangle when the embeddable is empty. In this case the workflow is to create an empty embeddable, add text like you would to a rectangle, then set the link.
author: zsviczian

body:
To me the whole use case of using alt text for export seems niche in that it doesn't add much value and very few people would use it _even_ if it was actually discoverable.

But if we are to support alt text, we should implement it in a proper way. Reusing bound text is not only undiscoverable, but also forces these weird workflows (the [1] doesn't work for me when the embed already has a url), and may/will have unintended consequences. We'd also have to maintain binding logic to work with embeds just for this.

So let's decide if we absolutely need alt text. I'm for dropping it, but if you really insist, then either:

1. add UI for it in the link editor
2. use some syntax which would still be undiscoverable but has some logic/precedent behind it. I propose using the CommonMark syntax for link titles: `[text "title"](url)` https://spec.commonmark.org/0.30/#link-title, by which I mean users would set the alt text by appending it in quotes into the url input.

I'd start with (2) especially because I'm not bought on the benefit/cost ratio yet.
author: dwelle

body: but just to clarify, my first go to would be dropping support altogether.
author: dwelle

body: We can drop it. I also don't think many people would use it, especially as it is not even obvious the feature is there. I am ok to drop it.

body:
Yeah, so the idea for this was that it would be up to the host app whether to enable this behaviour or not.

I discussed this with @maielo and we agreed that by default any canvas interaction should disable follow mode and this behaviour should be consistent across OSS and E+ for example.

But I personally still think making this configurable makes sense in some specific circumstances. One such idea was a *classroom environment* where students may follow a teacher around the canvas. In that case we could enable view mode for the followers as well as set this flag to false so that:
- students can't interfere with the teachers actions
- follow mode can still be disconnected explicitly by clicking on the teacher's avatar again or using the X on the badge at the bottom of the editor, but at least accidental clicks/scrolls won't disconnect the student implicitly which I think might be the desired behaviour in this instance

But if we think this is not really necessary and we don't really want to make this configurable, I'm not against removing it.

body:
In the perfect world we wouldn't have to capture & calculate the changes, but emit them directly to the store and let the store update the state. Bottom line for the tradeoff here is the rewrite complexity, as treating every single state update (even observable) as a command/event with payload and emitting it to the store isn't doable in one change.

Instead we we will have to reach that point incrementally & with this step we will be able to do it. In the end, we might also end up rewriting just some portions, such as hot paths due to perf. (i.e. to exchange uncommited increments of ephemeral actions) or unreliable parts (so we don't capture changes we don't want to capture, and don't end up in incosistent state after applying them).

body:
I rechecked reference props of other elements and it seems that we don't need have do this kind of merging (not to override other clients changes) in other cases, at least not now.

Closest next thing might be merging of linear element `points` - in case multiple clients modify the same linear  element and later undo / redo is perfomed to apply just changes. ATM one client will completely override the other clients changes, but it's even questionable if we should perform merge in such case as it might lead to completely broken linear element. Preferring one over the other throws the other clients changes away, but at least it leads to a consistent state which was define by one of the clients.

In the future, we might consider a special runtime brand or a decorator, which we would put over a given instance and it would automatically postprocess it (getting just the changes) & automatically merge the changes when applying it.
author: Mrazator

body:
As stated in https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/7348#discussion_r1521718445, we might offer a custom decorator / brand to merge extract just the changes of reference types, such as `customData` (perhaps based on some id) and merge only changes. However, right now it does not seem to be needed, so let's not over-engineer. 

For now basic shallow compare will be performed on the first level of `customData` props, if a difference will be found, whole `customData` will be recorded as part of the increment, in the state before (`deleted`) and after (`inserted`).

body:
This is questionable, since our remote elements got already restored indices (as part of restore) & were re-ordered (in reconciliation). Therefore for our multiplayer cases this is unnecessary. This means, that for most of our cases it adds one meaningless loop over all the elements. Other options we have:
* add `isRemoteUpdate` flag to `updateScene` to distinguish between remote and local updates which go through a different process and call `restoreFractionalIndices` only on local udpates
* force the host apps and local `updateScene` cases to always call `restoreElements` (which essentially calls `restoreFractionalIndices`) - this sounds weird, but goes in line with the logic of expecting already reconciled elements in `updateScene`
* add `reorderedElements` option to `updateScene` similar to what we are doing inside `replaceAllElements`, so that we don't accidentally override already correct indices with `restoreFractionalIndices`, but just fix the incorrect ones with `fixFractionalIndices` - problem is that this exposes internal behaviour outside
* leave as it is and potentially refactor away once we will be exchanging just changes between clients (or whatever else we will be doing)

For simplicity I would go with the last option for now, as none of them is ideal.

body:
previously we were refreshing only non-containerized text elements because we were afraid (and still are) that reflowing a containerized text could end up changing the container dimensions across different browsers (clients).

Instead, I decided to not refresh text dimensions at all in this function, and just invalidate all text elements (including bound text) so as to force re-render.

@Mrazator but, we have to come up with a strategy for whether or when should we refresh text dimensions. Especially with respect to the coming virgil update which will have slightly different text dimensions. In that case, do we want to refresh dimensions, or is it ok not do it and leave it only after users edit the text later (if at all).

---

But, I'm actually not sure about this even in the current case, because it may happen the virgil doesn't load for some reason and fall back to a serif font with wildly different dimensions. Should we then refresh dimensions or leave bad bounding boxes? Tough call.
author: dwelle

body:
As discussed on Discord, updating here:

1. We will keep old Virgil for backwards compatibility.
2. "it may happen the virgil doesn't load for some reason and fall back to a serif font with wildly different dimensions" - if we don't want to refresh dimensions because we are afraid of breaking container dimensions or the overall layout; then I would also not refresh dimensions at all if we are on a fallback font

## Useless regular-expression character escape

The escape sequence '\.' is equivalent to just '.', so the sequence may still represent a meta-character when it is used in a [regular expression](1).

[Show more details](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/security/code-scanning/21)

Also, we should consider a separate CDN for long-lived static assets, such as fonts.

I believe the code and responsibilities are clearer this way.
